items.title: Threat Actors Leverage Claude Code Leak as Social Engineering Lure to Distribute Malicious Payloads via GitHub
items.description: Cybercriminals are exploiting the recent Claude Code leak incident by using it as a social engineering tactic to deliver malware through GitHub repositories. The attackers have created trojanized versions of the leaked Claude source code, distributing malicious payloads including Vidar stealer version 18.7 and GhostSocks trojan. The campaign demonstrates rapid opportunistic exploitation of high-pr
items.ai_summary: Threat actors are leveraging the Claude code leak as a social engineering lure to distribute malicious payloads, including Vidar stealer and GhostSocks trojan, via trojanized GitHub repositories. This campaign demonstrates rapid opportunistic exploitation of high-profile incidents. Defenders should prioritize verifying the authenticity of GitHub repositories and code before executing or distributing them.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[User Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Threat Actor Targets Arabian Gulf Region With PlugX
items.description: In March 2026, a China-nexus threat actor launched a sophisticated campaign targeting countries in the Arabian Gulf region, exploiting renewed Middle East conflict themes within 24 hours of escalation. The attack utilized Arabic-language lures depicting missile strikes and employed a multi-stage infection chain beginning with weaponized ZIP archives containing malicious LNK and CHM files. The camp
items.ai_summary: The threat actor utilizes a multi-stage infection chain, starting with Arabic-language lures and weaponized ZIP archives, to target countries in the Arabian Gulf region. The attack involves various tactics, including user execution, stealing or forging authentication certificates, and data encryption. To defend against this threat, it is crucial to implement robust email filtering and user education to prevent the initial execution of malicious files.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1104| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1649| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Adobe Reader 0-day
items.description: On April 7, 2026, a security researcher described an Adobe Reader zero-day vulnerability that has been exploited since at least December 2025. The vulnerability allows threat actors to execute privileged Acrobat APIs via specially crafted malicious PDF files that execute obfuscated JavaScript when opened. Exploitation allows attackers to steal sensitive user and system data and to potentially laun
items.ai_summary: Adobe Reader 0-day vulnerability exploited via specially crafted malicious PDF files, allowing threat actors to execute privileged APIs, steal sensitive data, and potentially launch further attacks. This zero-day vulnerability has been exploited since at least December 2025. Defenders should immediately update Adobe Reader to the latest version.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566.001| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1649| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Tracking an OtterCookie Infostealer Campaign Across npm
items.description: Between April 6-9, 2026, multiple obfuscated malicious npm packages were identified as variants of the OtterCookie infostealer attributed to North Korean threat actors. The campaign employs a two-layer distribution strategy where benign wrapper packages clone legitimate libraries like big.js while pulling malicious dependencies containing the actual payload. Five malicious packages were identified
items.ai_summary: North Korean threat actors are using a two-layer distribution strategy to spread the OtterCookie infostealer via malicious npm packages. The campaign involves obfuscated files and supply chain compromise. Defenders should prioritize monitoring npm package dependencies for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Obfuscation Without Effort: Breaking a GIFTEDCROOK Stealer
items.description: A fresh GIFTEDCROOK stealer variant was identified as part of a UAC-0226 campaign targeting Ukraine. Initial access leverages CVE-2025-6218 and CVE-2025-8088 through a weaponized RAR archive containing a decoy PDF themed around military registry information. The attack chain uses an LNK file to execute obfuscated PowerShell code that decodes and deploys the payload. The stealer employs RC4 encrypt
items.ai_summary: A GIFTEDCROOK stealer variant is used in a UAC-0226 campaign targeting Ukraine, leveraging CVE-2025-6218 and CVE-2025-8088 for initial access. The attack chain employs obfuscated PowerShell code and RC4 encryption. Defenders should monitor for suspicious RAR archives and PowerShell activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1560| B[Archive Collected Data]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[PowerShell]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ASO RAT: Arabic-Language Android Surveillance Platform Targeting Syria
items.description: ASO RAT is a custom Android Remote Access Trojan featuring comprehensive device compromise capabilities including SMS interception, camera access, GPS tracking, call logging, file exfiltration, and DDoS functionality. Operating from Frankfurt-based infrastructure with connections to Syria, the platform disguises itself as PDF readers and Syrian government applications. Investigation revealed two a
items.ai_summary: ASO RAT is a custom Android Remote Access Trojan that compromises devices through masquerading as legitimate applications, allowing threat actors to intercept SMS, access cameras, and exfiltrate files. The real-world impact includes targeted surveillance of individuals in Syria. The most important defender action is to implement robust application vetting and validation processes to prevent the installation of malicious apps.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1036| B[Masquerading]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration Over C2 Channel]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: REFUNDEE: Inside a Shadow Panel Phishing-as-a-Service Operation
items.description: An open directory discovery at refundonex[.]com exposed a complete Phishing-as-a-Service and RAT-as-a-Service platform targeting Spanish and Portuguese-speaking victims. The investigation uncovered 3,788 files including weaponized LNK, VBS, and AES-encrypted PowerShell payloads delivering a remote access trojan. The platform, called Shadow Panel, operates from Bulgarian infrastructure and offers c
items.ai_summary: A Phishing-as-a-Service operation, called Shadow Panel, targets Spanish and Portuguese-speaking victims with weaponized payloads delivering a remote access trojan. The platform, hosted on Bulgarian infrastructure, offers various malicious services. Defenders should prioritize monitoring for T1566 phishing tactics and T1219 remote access software indicators.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

items.title: Fake recruiter campaign targets crypto developers with RAT
items.description: A sophisticated fake recruitment campaign named 'graphalgo' has been active since May 2025, targeting JavaScript and Python developers in the cryptocurrency sector. Attackers approach victims through LinkedIn, Facebook, and Reddit with fabricated job opportunities from fake blockchain companies like Veltrix Capital. The campaign uses malicious dependencies hidden in npm and PyPI packages, delivere
items.ai_summary: The 'graphalgo' campaign uses fake recruitment job opportunities to target crypto developers, delivering malicious dependencies via npm and PyPI packages. This leads to the deployment of Remote Access Software (RAT) via T1219. Defenders should monitor npm and PyPI package dependencies for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

items.title: Live C2 Dump Recovering Every Stage of the Kill Chain: CHM Dropper, VBScript Stager, PowerShell Keylogger
items.description: On April 11, 2026, researchers analyzed a CHM file (api_reference.chm) tagged as Kimsuky that initiated a three-stage attack chain. The C2 server at check[.]nid-log[.]com had directory listing enabled, allowing recovery of complete source code for all payload stages: a 6,338-byte VBScript performing system reconnaissance and establishing persistence via scheduled task, a 449-byte VBScript bridge t
items.ai_summary: Kimsuky threat actors exploited a CHM file to initiate a three-stage attack chain, recovering complete source code for all payload stages. The attack involved a VBScript dropper, a PowerShell stager, and a keylogger. Defenders should monitor for suspicious CHM files and PowerShell activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1053| C[Scheduled Task/Job]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[PowerShell]:::tactic
    D -->|T1056| E[Input Capture]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Polymarket Trader Funds at Risk: DPRK npm Package Steals Wallet Keys and Installs SSH Backdoor
items.description: On April 10, 2026, a malicious npm package named sleek-pretty@1.0.0 was published, targeting developers running automated trading bots on Polymarket, a prediction market platform with $477 million in open interest. The package executes four attack chains upon import: system fingerprinting, SSH backdoor installation on Linux hosts, filesystem exfiltration, and targeted theft of Polymarket CLOB API
items.ai_summary: A malicious npm package named sleek-pretty@1.0.0 was published, targeting Polymarket developers. The package executes four attack chains: system fingerprinting, SSH backdoor installation, filesystem exfiltration, and theft of Polymarket CLOB API keys. Defenders should immediately remove the package and update dependencies.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1592| B[Gather Victim Host Info]:::tactic
    B -->|T1195.001| C[Compromise Software Dependencies]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration Over C2 Channel]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-34775: Electron is a framework for writing cross-platform desktop applications using Ja...
items.description: Electron is a framework for writing cross-platform desktop applications using JavaScript, HTML and CSS. Prior to versions 38.8.6, 39.8.4, 40.8.4, and 41.0.0, the nodeIntegrationInWorker webPreference was not correctly scoped in all configurations. In certain process-sharing scenarios, workers spawned in frames configured with nodeIntegrationInWorker: false could still receive Node.js integration.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-34775 is a vulnerability in Electron framework allowing Node.js integration in workers even with nodeIntegrationInWorker set to false. This could lead to privilege escalation or data breach. Defenders should update Electron to versions 38.8.6, 39.8.4, 40.8.4, or 41.0.0.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1210| B[Reconnaissance]:::tactic
    B -->|T1110| C[Exploitation of Vulnerability]:::tactic
    C -->|T1068| D[Discovery]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-34774: Electron is a framework for writing cross-platform desktop applications using Ja...
items.description: Electron is a framework for writing cross-platform desktop applications using JavaScript, HTML and CSS. Prior to versions 39.8.1, 40.7.0, and 41.0.0, apps that use offscreen rendering and allow child windows via window.open() may be vulnerable to a use-after-free. If the parent offscreen WebContents is destroyed while a child window remains open, subsequent paint frames on the child dereference fr
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-34774 is a use-after-free vulnerability in Electron prior to versions 39.8.1, 40.7.0, and 41.0.0. This vulnerability allows attackers to exploit offscreen rendering and child windows via window.open(). To defend, update Electron to a patched version.

items.title: CVE-2026-34773: Electron is a framework for writing cross-platform desktop applications using Ja...
items.description: Electron is a framework for writing cross-platform desktop applications using JavaScript, HTML and CSS. Prior to versions 38.8.6, 39.8.1, 40.8.1, and 41.0.0, on Windows, app.setAsDefaultProtocolClient(protocol) did not validate the protocol name before writing to the registry. Apps that pass untrusted input as the protocol name may allow an attacker to write to arbitrary subkeys under HKCU\Softwar
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-34773 is a vulnerability in Electron that allows an attacker to write to arbitrary subkeys under HKCU\Softwar by passing untrusted input as the protocol name. This can be exploited by apps that use Electron prior to versions 38.8.6, 39.8.1, 40.8.1, and 41.0.0. To defend, ensure Electron is updated to a patched version.

items.title: CVE-2026-34772: Electron is a framework for writing cross-platform desktop applications using Ja...
items.description: Electron is a framework for writing cross-platform desktop applications using JavaScript, HTML and CSS. Prior to versions 38.8.6, 39.8.0, 40.7.0, and 41.0.0-beta.8, apps that allow downloads and programmatically destroy sessions may be vulnerable to a use-after-free. If a session is torn down while a native save-file dialog is open for a download, dismissing the dialog dereferences freed memory, w
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-34772 is a use-after-free vulnerability in Electron applications that handle downloads and programmatically destroy sessions. An attacker could exploit this by tricking a user into initiating a download, leading to a crash or potential code execution. The most important defender action is to update Electron to patched versions and audit applications for proper session management during downloads.

items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-34771 is a use-after-free vulnerability in Electron prior to versions 38.8.6, 39.8.0, 40.7.0, and 41.0.0-beta.8. items.title: Scans for EncystPHP Webshell, (Mon, Apr 13th)
items.description: Last week, I wrote about attackers scanning for various webshells, hoping to find some that do not require authentication or others that use well-known credentials. But some attackers are paying attention and are deploying webshells with more difficult-to-guess credentials. Today, I noticed some scans for what appears to be the "EncystPHP" web shell. Fortinet wrote about this webshell back in January. It appears to be a favorite among attackers compromising vulnerable FreePBX systems.

items.title: Number Usage in Passwords: Take Two, (Thu, Apr 9th)
items.description: In a previous diary [1], we looked to see how numbers were used within passwords submitted to honeypots. One of the items of interest was how dates, and more specifically years, were represented within the data and how that changed over time. It is often seen that years and seasons are used in passwords, especially when password change requirements include frequenty password changes. Some examples we might see today:

items.title: A Little Bit Pivoting: What Web Shells are Attackers Looking for?, (Tue, Apr 7th)
items.description: Webshells remain a popular method for attackers to maintain persistence on a compromised web server. Many "arbitrary file write" and "remote code execution" vulnerabilities are used to drop small files on systems for later execution of additional payloads. The names of these files keep changing and are often chosen to "fit in" with other files. Webshells themselves are also often used by parasitic attacks to compromise a server. Sadly (?), attackers are not always selecting good passwords either. In some cases, webshells come with pre-set backdoor credentials, which may be overlooked by a less sophisticated attacker.
items.workflow_graph:

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: ‘Starkiller’ Phishing Service Proxies Real Login Pages, MFA
items.description: Most phishing websites are little more than static copies of login pages for popular online destinations, and they are often quickly taken down by anti-abuse activists and security firms. But a stealthy new phishing-as-a-service offering lets customers sidestep both of these pitfalls: It uses cleverly disguised links to load the target brand's real website, and then acts as a relay between the target and the legitimate site -- forwarding the victim's username, password and multi-factor authentication (MFA) code to the legitimate site and returning its responses.

items.title: Kimwolf Botnet Swamps Anonymity Network I2P
items.description: For the past week, the massive "Internet of Things" (IoT) botnet known as Kimwolf has been disrupting the The Invisible Internet Project (I2P), a decentralized, encrypted communications network designed to anonymize and secure online communications. I2P users started reporting disruptions in the network around the same time the Kimwolf botmasters began relying on it to evade takedown attempts against the botnet's control servers.
